title: The Virtues
html:
The Big Question

What transforms an ordinary person into someone capable of giving their life for the truth?

On July 6, 1535, a man climbed the scaffold at the Tower of London. He had been Chancellor of England —the second-highest office in the kingdom— a humanist, jurist, writer, personal friend of Henry VIII, and father of four beloved children. He had everything the world can offer: power, money, prestige, a beautiful family, and the favor of the most powerful monarch in Europe. But that day, Thomas More lost his head —literally— because he refused to sign an oath that would betray his conscience.

The film A Man for All Seasons (1966) depicts those final months with almost theological precision. There is a scene in which his daughter Margaret visits him in his cell and begs him to yield: "Father, God wouldn't want you to die. Sign, and then think whatever you want in your heart." And More answers with a line that defines this entire lesson: "When a man makes an oath, he is holding himself in his own hands, like water; and if he opens his fingers, he need not hope to find himself again."

What sustained Thomas More? Not stubbornness. Not pride. It was virtues. Prudence to discern what was at stake. Justice to recognize that the king could not usurp the authority of the Pope. Fortitude to stand firm when everyone —even those he loved— urged him to yield. And a virtue he did not give himself: the faith, hope, and charity that God had infused into his soul at baptism.

The Catechism of the Catholic Church dedicates a dense and luminous section to the virtues (CCC 1803-1845). It defines them as "a habitual and firm disposition to do the good" (CCC 1803). And it distinguishes two great families: the human virtues —which we can acquire through effort and grace— and the theological virtues —which only God can infuse. This distinction is not an academic detail: it is the difference between a Stoic who masters himself and a saint who lets himself be possessed by God.

This lesson covers the complete map of the virtues: the four cardinal ones that the ancients already knew (prudence, justice, fortitude, and temperance), the three theological ones that only Christianity reveals (faith, hope, and charity), the gifts of the Holy Spirit that perfect them, and the fruits that bloom when the tree of grace takes root in a concrete life.

Activity

Imagine you are an athlete. Nobody is born a champion: you have to train every day, repeat movements, eat well, rest, listen to the coach. Virtues are like that: good habits that grow stronger with practice. But there is one difference: in sports, the strength comes from you alone. In the Christian life, it is God who gives you the most important strength (grace). You contribute the effort; He provides the power.

Draw a tree with deep roots. In the roots write: "God gives me the strength." On the trunk write: "I practice the good." On the branches write the virtues you would like to have. Ask: what can I do TODAY to help this virtue grow, even just a little?

1. What Is a Virtue? The Habitual Disposition to Do Good

The Catechism opens the section with a definition worth reading slowly: "A virtue is an habitual and firm disposition to do the good" (CCC 1803). Every word carries weight:

  • Disposition: not an isolated act, but a stable orientation of the person. It is not "today I was kind" but "I am a kind person."
  • Habitual: it has become custom, second nature. Like a pianist who no longer thinks about where the keys are —his fingers go there on their own.
  • Firm: it resists pressure. It is not a fair-weather disposition. Like the anchor of a ship in a storm.
  • To do the good: virtue is not neutral. It aims at the moral good, at what perfects the person. There is no "virtue" for robbing banks efficiently.

The Catechism adds that virtue allows the person "not only to perform good acts, but to give the best of himself." With all his sensible and spiritual powers, the virtuous person tends toward the good, chooses it, and realizes it in his concrete actions (CCC 1803).

Acquired Virtues and Infused Virtues

Here is an important theological distinction that the Catechism presupposes but does not always make explicit:

Acquired virtue: obtained through the repetition of good acts (with the help of God's actual grace). Like a muscle developed at the gym. The cardinal virtues can be acquired in this way, which is why virtuous pagans (Socrates, Marcus Aurelius) possessed them to some degree.

Infused virtue: poured out by God directly into the soul, without our acts being able to produce it. The theological virtues are always infused (they are received at baptism). And God can also infuse the supernatural cardinal virtues, which are of the same type as the acquired ones but elevated to a new order: they no longer seek merely human good, but union with God.

St. Thomas Aquinas explains this duality with a luminous analogy: acquired temperance moderates the desire for food in order to preserve health; infused temperance moderates that same desire to make us fit for the contemplation of God (STh I-II, q.63, a.3). Same external act, radically different motivation.

Context

In the ancient world, the Greek word areté (virtue) meant "excellence." A horse had areté if it ran fast; a warrior, if he fought with courage. The philosophers —Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, the Stoics— transferred this concept to the soul: virtue is the excellence of the human being, that which makes him function according to his nature. Aristotle defined virtue as an elective habit consisting in a mean between two vices: courage (fortitude) between cowardice and recklessness; generosity between avarice and prodigality (Nicomachean Ethics, Book II).

Christianity does not reject this heritage. It assumes it, purifies it, and elevates it. What Aristotle intuited through reason, Revelation confirms with the light of God. But it adds something radically new: there are virtues that are not within the reach of human effort. Faith, hope, and charity are not acquired through training. They are gift. And that gift changes everything else.

2. The Cardinal Virtues: The Four Hinges of the Moral Life

The term "cardinal" comes from the Latin cardo, cardinis: hinge, pivot. The cardinal virtues are the hinges upon which the whole moral life turns. The Catechism presents them thus: "Four virtues play a pivotal role. For this reason they are called 'cardinal'; all the others are grouped around them" (CCC 1805).

Their oldest biblical source is found in a book that tradition attributes to Solomon:

"She [Wisdom] teaches temperance and prudence, justice and fortitude: the most useful things for men in life." (Wis 8:7)

Four words that the Greeks already knew (phrónesis, dikaiosýne, andreía, sophrosýne) but which divine Wisdom claims as her own. Let us review each one.

2.1 Prudence: The Charioteer of the Virtues

The Catechism defines it as "the virtue that disposes practical reason to discern our true good in every circumstance and to choose the right means for achieving it" (CCC 1806). St. Thomas called it recta ratio agibilium — right reason in acting— and the tradition has given it an incomparable title: the charioteer of the virtues (auriga virtutum).

The image is exact: the charioteer drives the chariot. Without prudence, fortitude becomes recklessness, justice becomes rigidity, temperance becomes pusillanimity. Prudence is not the "caution" of one who never risks anything; it is the practical intelligence of one who knows what is fitting to do here and now.

Thomas More was an eminently prudent man. He did not rush into public opposition: he kept silence as long as he could, sought every legal loophole, did not say one word too many. But when silence ceased to be enough, he spoke. And then his prudence revealed itself for what it was: not fear disguised, but genuine discernment.

The Catechism adds: "The prudent man looks where he is going" (Prov 14:15). "Be sober and vigilant for prayer" (1 Pet 4:7). Christian prudence is not worldly calculation; it is reason enlightened by faith that discerns what pleases the Lord.

2.2 Justice: To Give Each Their Due

"Justice is the moral virtue that consists in the constant and firm will to give their due to God and neighbor" (CCC 1807). The classical formula, which comes from the Roman jurist Ulpian, is suum cuique tribuere: to give each their own.

But the Catechism introduces a decisive expansion here: justice is not only toward neighbor. It is also —and primarily— toward God. It is called the virtue of religion: to give God the worship that is His due, to acknowledge Him as Creator, to adore Him. A person can be scrupulously just with their fellow human beings and yet live as if God did not exist. That is not full justice.

St. Thomas More embodied justice in both senses. As judge and chancellor, he was famous for his fairness: he dispatched cases with a speed that no chancellor had achieved, and no one could ever accuse him of corruption. But when Henry VIII demanded that he recognize the spiritual supremacy of the king over the Pope, More understood that what was at stake was not politics but religion: giving God what belongs to God. And he did not yield.

2.3 Fortitude: Firmness unto Martyrdom

"Fortitude is the moral virtue that ensures firmness in difficulties and constancy in the pursuit of the good" (CCC 1808). The definition is brief but the reality is immense: fortitude is not aggressiveness nor the absence of fear; it is the capacity to remain firm when fear exists and is legitimate.

The Catechism goes so far as to say that fortitude "enables one even to accept the sacrifice of one's own life in defense of a just cause." It is the virtue of the martyrs. More climbed the scaffold with a serenity that left witnesses astonished. His last words were: "I die the King's good servant, but God's first." There is the correct hierarchy: fortitude is not heroic insensitivity; it is ordered love.

St. John Paul II —who canonized Thomas More in 1935 as a martyr and declared him patron of statesmen and politicians in the year 2000— lived this same fortitude in the 20th century: resisting Nazism and communism, surviving an assassination attempt, and governing the Church for 26 years with a Parkinson's disease that stripped him of everything except dignity.

2.4 Temperance: Mastery over the Instincts

"Temperance is the moral virtue that moderates the attraction of pleasures and provides balance in the use of created goods" (CCC 1809). In a world that has made immediate pleasure its god, temperance is countercultural by definition. But it is also liberating: one who does not govern himself is a slave to his appetites.

The Catechism stresses that temperance "ensures the will's mastery over instincts and keeps desires within the limits of what is honorable." It is not about annihilating desires but ordering them. The temperate person enjoys created goods without being trapped by them. He eats without gluttony, drinks without drunkenness, rests without sloth, loves without possessiveness.

More lived temperance in its highest form: mastery over the instinct of self-preservation. What is natural is to cling to life. He gave it up —not because he despised it, but because there was something worth more.

Connection

C. S. Lewis dedicated an entire chapter of Mere Christianity to the cardinal virtues. His argument is brilliant: a Christian is not someone who rejects pagan morality but someone who completes it. Lewis says the four cardinal virtues are like the four points of a compass: they point toward the moral north even for someone who does not know Christ. But then the three theological virtues arrive and they are like a new map revealing a territory that the compass alone cannot reach.

Lewis insists that prudence is not "being a killjoy" but thinking before acting. Justice is not only giving money to the poor but also giving them truth, respect, and time. Fortitude includes physical and moral courage: "the one who endures boredom and injustice without giving up." And temperance is not abolishing pleasure but putting it in its place. For Lewis, vice is not "going too far" but "putting a pleasure in the place that only God should occupy."

3. The Theological Virtues: The Soul Grafted into God

So far we have discussed virtues that human reason can reach —with effort and grace. But Christianity introduces a radical novelty: there are three virtues that we cannot acquire by ourselves. They are infused by God at baptism. They are called theological because they have God as their origin, motive, and object.

The Catechism says so: the theological virtues "are infused by God into the souls of the faithful to make them capable of acting as his children and of meriting eternal life. They are the pledge of the presence and action of the Holy Spirit in the faculties of the human being" (CCC 1813). There are three: faith, hope, and charity.

3.1 Faith: Adherence to God Who Reveals Himself

"Faith is the theological virtue by which we believe in God and believe all that he has said and revealed to us, and that Holy Church proposes for our belief, because he is truth itself" (CCC 1814).

Faith is not credulity nor optimism. It is the free response of the human person to a God who took the initiative to reveal himself. By faith, the person abandons himself entirely into the hands of God. But that abandonment is not blind: it is illuminated by the certainty that the one who speaks is Truth itself.

More had faith. Not a blurry or cultural faith, but a faith that had been tested in prayer, in the reading of Scripture, in the sacraments. When the moment of truth came, that faith did not crumble.

3.2 Hope: Trust in God's Promises

"Hope is the theological virtue by which we desire the kingdom of heaven and eternal life as our happiness, placing our trust in Christ's promises and relying not on our own strength, but on the help of the grace of the Holy Spirit" (CCC 1817).

Hope is not optimism. The optimist expects things to turn out well because he trusts in human resources. The Christian hopes because he trusts in God's promises. The difference is enormous: optimism collapses when circumstances are adverse; hope is sustained even in failure, in sickness, and in death.

Thomas More was not an optimist about his immediate destiny: he knew he was going to die. But he had hope. He knew that death was not the end. His letters from the Tower of London are full of this serene hope: not of someone deceiving himself, but of someone who looks beyond.

3.3 Charity: The Love with Which God Loves Himself in Us

"Charity is the theological virtue by which we love God above all things for his own sake, and our neighbor as ourselves for the love of God" (CCC 1822).

St. Paul dedicates the most celebrated text of Christian literature to it:

"If I speak in the tongues of men and of angels, but have not love, I am a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal... Love is patient and kind; love is not jealous or boastful; it is not arrogant or rude... So faith, hope, love abide, these three; but the greatest of these is love." (1 Cor 13:1,4,13)

Charity is the "form" of all the virtues. That is to say: it animates them, unites them among themselves, orders them. Without charity, prudence is cunning, justice is calculation, fortitude is stubbornness, temperance is coldness. With charity, the Christian life becomes what it should be: love responding to Love.

Mother Teresa of Calcutta is the modern icon of charity in action. She did not talk about theology; she picked up the dying from the streets. But that gesture was theology in action. She explained it: "I do not serve the poor because they are good, but because in them I see Christ thirsty, hungry, naked." Charity is not philanthropy. It is seeing Christ in the other and acting accordingly.

Activity

Think of someone with whom it is hard for you to be patient or kind. Write their name on a piece of paper. During this week, every time you are about to speak about him or her, say a brief prayer: "Lord, give me charity for this person." It is not about suddenly liking them. It is about loving them because God loves them. That is charity.

4. The Gifts of the Holy Spirit and the Fruits of the Virtuous Life

The Catechism teaches that the virtues are not sufficient on their own. They need to be elevated, perfected, and that is done by the gifts of the Holy Spirit (CCC 1830-1831). There are seven: wisdom, understanding, counsel, fortitude, knowledge, piety, and fear of the Lord.

The gifts are not extraordinary abilities for a chosen few. They are permanent dispositions received by every baptized person that make the soul docile to follow the promptings of the Holy Spirit. Like the sails of a ship that catch the wind and convert it into movement. Without the gifts, the virtues are human rowing; with the gifts, they are navigation assisted by the divine Wind.

And when the Holy Spirit acts through the virtues and the gifts, the fruits of the Spirit are produced. St. Paul enumerates them:

"But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control; against such there is no law." (Gal 5:22-23)

The Latin Vulgate and the tradition of the Catechism enumerate twelve fruits: charity, joy, peace, patience, longanimity, goodness, benignity, gentleness, faithfulness, modesty, continence, and chastity (CCC 1832). These fruits are the visible proof that grace is working. They are the "flavor" that a life inhabited by God leaves behind.

St. John Paul II, in his encyclical Veritatis Splendor (1993), stressed that the virtues are not a decorative option but the skeleton of the moral life. Without them, morality becomes a list of prohibitions; with them, it is a path of plenitude.

Context

Virtue, character, and habit are intimately connected. Habit is the repetition of acts that gradually configures a stable disposition. Character is the ensemble of those dispositions converted into identity. Virtue is the habit oriented to the good. The virtuous person does not do good because she feels like it every morning, but because she has trained herself to do it until it becomes what she is.

The Stoics understood this admirably. Marcus Aurelius, the philosopher-emperor, wrote in his Meditations: "At dawn, when you have trouble getting out of bed, tell yourself: I have to go to work — as a human being." But Stoicism relies on the strength of the will alone. Christianity adds grace: God not only commands us to be virtuous; He gives us the power to be so. The difference between a pagan sage and a Christian saint is the difference between an athlete who trains alone and one who trains with a coach who has also given him the lungs.

5. The Path of the Virtues: Scripture Illuminating the Way

Scripture is not an abstract catalog of virtues. It is the map of a path that begins in grace and ends in glory. St. Peter condenses it in a cascade of verbs:

"For this very reason, make every effort to supplement your faith with virtue, and virtue with knowledge, and knowledge with self-control, and self-control with steadfastness, and steadfastness with godliness, and godliness with brotherly affection, and brotherly affection with love. For if these things are yours and abound, they keep you from being ineffective or unfruitful in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ." (2 Pet 1:5-8)

It is like a ladder: each virtue prepares the next. Faith is the first rung; charity is the last. And the whole edifice is sustained by the grace of God.

St. Paul, for his part, offers the "golden rule of virtuous thinking":

"Finally, brethren, whatever is true, whatever is honorable, whatever is just, whatever is pure, whatever is lovely, whatever is gracious, if there is any virtue, and if there is any praise, think about these things." (Phil 4:8)

It is the pedagogy of attention: what you contemplate transforms you. If your mind chews on the bad, you become cynical; if it contemplates the good, the beautiful, and the true, you become virtuous. Virtue does not begin in action but in the gaze.

For Reflection

1. Is there a difficult decision in your life right now in which you need prudence (to discern the concrete good) and fortitude (to remain firm)?

Take five minutes to identify it. The goal is not to resolve it here. It is to name it and ask for the light of the Holy Spirit.

2. Of the four cardinal virtues, which do you think is your strongest point and which your weakest?

Be honest with yourself. We all have an imbalance. Recognizing it is the first step toward correcting it.

3. Faith, hope, and charity are gifts. Do you treat your virtues as personal achievements or as gifts that God has given you and that need to be cared for?

If your answer is "achievements": beware of spiritual pride. If it is "gifts": are you giving them the maintenance they deserve?

4. Is there anyone to whom you owe justice? (An apology, a thank-you, a recognition you have not given them.)

Justice begins in the concrete. Not in great causes, but in the small debts of love toward those who live beside you.

5. Which fruit of the Holy Spirit would you like to see grow more in your life this week? (Peace, patience, gentleness, self-control...)

Choose one only. Ask for it each morning. And stay alert to opportunities to practice it during the day.

Key Idea

The virtues are not a list of duties to fulfill. They are the muscles of the soul. They are acquired through training, sustained by grace, and their ultimate goal is not to make us "good people" but to make us capable of God: capable of loving Him, trusting in Him, and serving Him in our neighbor. The cardinal virtues provide the skeleton; the theological virtues provide the soul. And the gifts and fruits of the Holy Spirit are the evidence that God himself is working in you.

Cultural Wealth

Film A Man for All Seasons (Fred Zinnemann, 1966). Winner of six Academy Awards including Best Picture. Paul Scofield portrays Thomas More with a sobriety that leaves one breathless. The film functions as a visual meditation on the virtues: every dialogue is a lesson in prudence, justice, fortitude, and faith. Essential viewing for this lesson.

Literature C. S. Lewis, Mere Christianity, Book III, Chapter 2: "The Cardinal Virtues." Lewis explains the four virtues with the clarity of an Oxford professor and the grace of a convert. He says that prudence, justice, fortitude, and temperance are "things everyone recognizes as good," but adds that a Christian must practice them for different reasons and with a strength that is not his own. Available in English and Spanish at bookstores and libraries.

Testimony St. John Paul II, Veritatis Splendor (1993), Chapter II. The encyclical on the foundations of Catholic morality develops the role of the virtues as the human person's response to God's call. Also his apostolic letter Mulieris Dignitatem (1988) on the dignity of women, where the Pope reflects on fortitude in love. Available at vatican.va.

Art Hans Holbein the Younger, Portrait of Thomas More (1527, Frick Collection, New York). The painting captures More at the height of his career, before the conflict. Intelligent gaze, firm hands, the chain of office —all the power of the world hanging from his neck— and yet there is something in his expression that suggests he has already done the calculation and knows what each thing is worth.

Music John Rutter, Requiem and sacred choral works. Many of his pieces draw from the Christian tradition of hope and trust in God. Also Matt Maher, "Lord, I Need You" — a contemporary expression of the dependence on grace that underlies every virtue. In the classical realm, Bach's Jesu, Joy of Man's Desiring is a musical rendering of virtue as joy rather than burden.

Youth Catechism YouCat, questions 299-311. The section on the virtues in YouCat is one of its best executed: it explains each cardinal virtue, the theological virtues, and the gifts of the Holy Spirit with biblical quotations, concrete examples, and questions for personal reflection. Available for free at youcat.org.

Audiovisual Catechesis The Bible Project (YouTube), video "The Holy Spirit" and the series on the fruits of the Spirit. Animations of the highest theological and visual quality, available in multiple languages. Also Ascension Presents with Fr. Mike Schmitz: the episode "How to Grow in Virtue" explains the connection between habit, character, and grace with everyday examples. The Word on Fire channel with Bishop Robert Barron also has excellent content on virtue and character.

Comparative Philosophy Stoic Virtues vs. Christian Virtues. The Stoics (Seneca, Epictetus, Marcus Aurelius) cultivated prudence, justice, fortitude, and temperance as a path toward ataraxia (imperturbable serenity). Christianity assumes those same virtues but redirects them: not toward the self-sufficiency of the sage, but toward loving dependence on God. The Stoic masters himself; the Christian lets himself be mastered by Christ. The difference is humility. Also, the Hagakure and the samurai code (Bushido) exalt fortitude, but a fortitude for death without the hope of resurrection. Christian fortitude, in contrast, rests on the certainty that Christ has already conquered death.

Opus Dei The series "Themes of Christian Faith" at opusdei.org. The themes on the theological and cardinal virtues offer a deep and accessible synthesis, with references to the Catechism and texts from the founder of Opus Dei, St. Josemaría Escrivá. Recommended for catechists who want to go deeper.
